articles.title: Downed or Sparking Power Line Response
articles.content: A downed, low-hanging, or sparking power line is always treated as energized and life-threatening. Triage steps: 1. Tell the caller to stay at least 35 feet away and keep others and vehicles back. 2. Do not touch the line, anything it contacts, or any person in contact with it. 3. If the line is on a vehicle, advise occupants to stay inside unless there is fire. 4. Classify as FIELD_HAZARD and route to field-operations; add a life-safety escalation hint if anyone is near the line, it is arcing, or it has ignited vegetation. 5. Capture exact location (cross streets or pole number) and whether the road or sidewalk is blocked. 6. Open or attach to an incident and notify the utility's emergency line. Never advise the public to move a line themselves.
articles.snippet: Treat every downed line as energized: keep 35+ feet away, never touch it, route to field-operations, and escalate if anyone is near it or it is arcing.

articles.title: Widespread Power Outage Intake and ETR
articles.content: For reports of lost power affecting a block, neighborhood, or larger area: 1. Confirm scope - single premise, block, or wide area - and ask when power was lost. 2. Note corroborating signals (transformer hum stopped, loud bang, flicker before loss, neighbors also dark) which raise confidence and magnitude. 3. Classify as INFRASTRUCTURE_OUTAGE and route to engineering. 4. Attach the report to an existing incident when the location and timing match an open outage so magnitude (number of reports) increments instead of opening duplicates. 5. Provide the current Estimated Time of Restoration (ETR) if one exists; if not, state that crews are assessing and an ETR will follow. 6. Advise the caller to report any downed lines or hazards separately as life-safety. Do not promise a restoration time the engineering queue has not published.
articles.snippet: Confirm outage scope and start time, route to engineering, attach matching reports to the open incident, and share the published ETR or say crews are assessing.

articles.title: Gas Leak or Odor of Gas
articles.content: A reported gas odor, hissing line, or carbon-monoxide-style symptom is a life-safety emergency. Triage steps: 1. Instruct the caller to leave the building or area immediately and move upwind to fresh air, taking others with them. 2. Tell them NOT to operate light switches, appliances, phones, or anything that could create a spark while inside, and not to start vehicles in an attached garage. 3. Once safely outside and away, contact 911 and the gas utility's emergency line. 4. Classify as PUBLIC_SAFETY, route to field-operations, and set requires_escalation = true (escalation_reason = life_safety). 5. Capture address, number of people affected, and whether anyone reports dizziness, nausea, or unconsciousness. Never advise the caller to locate or stop the leak themselves.
articles.snippet: Gas leaks are life-safety: get everyone out and upwind, no sparks or switches, call 911 and the gas utility, then route to field-operations and escalate.

articles.title: Structure Fire or Heavy Smoke Reported
articles.content: For any report of fire, heavy smoke, or possible people trapped: 1. Confirm everyone is getting out and staying out; tell the caller never to re-enter a burning structure. 2. Ensure 911 has been called; place the call for them if needed. 3. Classify as PUBLIC_SAFETY, route to field-operations, set requires_escalation = true (escalation_reason = life_safety) and priority urgent. 4. Capture address, number of floors, whether anyone may be trapped or injured, and any hazards (gas, chemicals, electrical). 5. Open a high-priority incident immediately - never wait for business hours or a callback. Surface emergency guidance in the response before anything else.
articles.snippet: Fire or trapped people: get out and stay out, confirm 911 is called, route to field-operations, escalate as life-safety, and open a high-priority incident now.

articles.title: Water Main Break or Flooding
articles.content: For a burst water main, street flooding, or rapidly rising water: 1. Advise the caller to avoid driving or walking through moving or standing water - it may hide downed lines, sinkholes, or debris. 2. If water is near electrical equipment or a downed line is in the water, treat it as a life-safety hazard and escalate. 3. Classify as FIELD_HAZARD and route to field-operations. 4. Capture location, how fast water is rising, whether roads are impassable, and whether any structures are taking on water. 5. Open or attach to an incident; if the break threatens a road, structure, or the electrical system, add an escalation hint. Note any boil-water or service-interruption implications for customer-comms follow-up.
articles.snippet: Water main break or flooding: keep people out of moving water, watch for energized water near downed lines, and route to field-operations (escalate if it hits the grid).

articles.title: Transformer or Substation Fault
articles.content: Reports of a loud bang or boom, a bright blue/green flash, smoke from a pole-top can, or a substation alarm indicate equipment failure. Triage steps: 1. Confirm whether power is out and whether there is any fire, smoke, or downed conductor at the scene. 2. If there is fire or a downed line, treat the FIELD_HAZARD / PUBLIC_SAFETY aspect as primary and escalate. 3. Otherwise classify as INFRASTRUCTURE_OUTAGE and route to engineering. 4. Capture the pole number or substation name, time of the event, and the extent of any outage. 5. Attach to an existing outage incident if the location and timing match. Equipment-failure reports help engineering localize the fault and refine the ETR.
articles.snippet: Bang, flash, or smoke from a transformer/substation: check for fire or downed lines first (escalate), otherwise route the outage to engineering with pole/substation ID.

articles.title: Blocked Road, Fallen Tree, or Debris
articles.content: For a fallen tree, large debris, or a blocked roadway: 1. Ask whether any power lines are tangled in the tree or debris - if so, treat it as a downed-line life-safety hazard and escalate, and keep the public 35+ feet away. 2. If no lines are involved, classify as FIELD_HAZARD and route to field-operations. 3. Capture the exact location, whether the road is fully or partially blocked, and whether anyone is injured or trapped. 4. Note if the obstruction is blocking emergency access or a critical route, which raises priority. 5. Open or attach to an incident. Advise the caller not to attempt to clear lines or heavy debris themselves.
articles.snippet: Fallen tree or debris: check for entangled power lines first (escalate, keep clear), otherwise route the road hazard to field-operations with exact location.

articles.title: Billing and Account Inquiries
articles.content: For questions about a bill, charge, payment, or account: 1. Classify as CUSTOMER_INQUIRY and route to customer-comms. 2. Do not collect or repeat full card numbers, bank details, or Social Security numbers; if a caller volunteers them, redact and never echo them back. 3. For disputed charges, high balances, or hardship, capture the account reference and a brief summary so customer-comms can follow up; flag for escalation only if the caller explicitly asks for a person or the matter is a hardship/shutoff issue. 4. Routine account changes (mailing address, paperless billing, payment arrangements) are SERVICE_REQUEST and also go to customer-comms. 5. Never promise a credit, refund, or rate change the customer-comms team has not approved.
articles.snippet: Billing, charges, and account changes route to customer-comms. Never repeat full card/SSN details; capture an account reference and summarize for follow-up.

articles.title: Outage Status and Incident Follow-Up
articles.content: When a caller is checking on an existing outage or a report they already filed: 1. Classify as STATUS_CHECK and route to customer-comms. 2. If they reference an incident or ticket ID, read it back character by character to confirm; for voice, spell IDs out (for example, I-N-C dash one zero two four). 3. Look up the incident and share its current status and published ETR; if no ETR exists, say crews are assessing and one will follow. 4. If the caller is reporting a NEW symptom (a hazard, a spreading outage), reclassify and route accordingly rather than only updating status. 5. Offer to attach their confirmation to the existing incident so magnitude reflects the true number of affected customers.
articles.snippet: Status checks route to customer-comms: confirm the incident ID, share current status and ETR, and reclassify if the caller is actually reporting something new.

articles.title: Regulatory and Statutory Incident Reporting Windows
articles.content: Certain events carry a legal reporting clock and must reach the compliance desk promptly: product recalls, data or privacy breaches, hazardous-material releases, serious injuries, and regulator-mandated notifications. Triage steps: 1. Classify as COMPLIANCE_REPORT and route to compliance-desk. 2. Set requires_escalation = true with escalation_reason = statutory_clock whenever a reporting deadline applies. 3. Capture the event type, when it occurred or was discovered, and any deadline the caller cites. 4. Do not attempt to interpret the regulation or advise on legal sufficiency - that is the compliance desk's role. 5. Open an incident immediately so the statutory window is preserved with a timestamp; never let a compliance matter sit in a routine queue.
articles.snippet: Recalls, breaches, hazmat releases, and mandated notifications go to compliance-desk and escalate on the statutory clock; open the incident at once to preserve the window.

articles.title: Carbon Monoxide Alarm or Symptoms
articles.content: A sounding carbon monoxide (CO) alarm, or callers reporting headache, dizziness, nausea, or drowsiness affecting multiple people in one location, is a life-safety emergency. Triage steps: 1. Get everyone outside to fresh air immediately, including pets, and do a head count. 2. Do not re-enter until emergency responders say it is safe. 3. Ensure 911 is called; place the call if the caller cannot. 4. Classify as PUBLIC_SAFETY, route to field-operations, set requires_escalation = true (life_safety). 5. Capture address, number of people affected, and who is symptomatic. CO is odorless - take every alarm seriously even if no one feels ill yet.
articles.snippet: CO alarm or multiple people with headache/dizziness: get everyone out and into fresh air, call 911, route to field-operations, and escalate as life-safety.

articles.title: Requesting a Human or Escalating to a Dispatcher
articles.content: When a caller explicitly asks for a person, a manager, or a live dispatcher, honor the request. Triage steps: 1. Classify as HUMAN_REQUEST and route to escalations; set requires_escalation = true (escalation_reason = user_requested_human). 2. Acknowledge the request directly and let the caller know a human will follow up - do not loop them back through automated triage. 3. Capture a brief reason and the best callback number so the escalations queue has context. 4. If the underlying issue is a life-safety hazard, the safety classification takes precedence and is handled as urgent regardless of the human request. 5. Always escalate clear distress, threats of harm, or sensitive safety topics to a human immediately, even if not explicitly requested.
articles.snippet: Explicit requests for a person route to escalations and escalate as user_requested_human; acknowledge directly, capture a callback, and never loop them back through triage.
